pathophysiology_summary: Acute pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardium (the sac around the heart), most commonly viral/idiopathic, often 1-3 weeks after a viral illness. The inflamed, roughened pericardial layers produce sharp, pleuritic retrosternal pain that is characteristically worse lying flat and on deep inspiration (which stretches/irritates the pericardium) and relieved by sitting up and leaning forward (reducing contact/tension), with radiation to the trapezius ridges (referred via the phrenic nerve, which is fairly specific for pericardial irritation). The auscultatory hallmark is a pericardial friction rub - a high-pitched, scratching, often triphasic sound best heard at the left lower sternal border with the patient leaning forward in expiration. The diagnosis is clinical, supported by widespread (diffuse) concave ST-segment elevation and PR-segment depression on ECG (distinct from the territorial ST elevation of STEMI), and often a raised CRP; troponin is checked because concurrent myocardial involvement (myopericarditis) raises acuity. Uncomplicated viral pericarditis is generally benign and self-limiting, treated with high-dose NSAIDs plus colchicine (with gastric protection), with restriction of strenuous exercise - importantly, NSAIDs ARE first-line here, in contrast to isolated myocarditis where they are relatively contraindicated. The important complications to exclude are acute coronary syndrome (the key mimic; pain crushing rather than positional), massive pulmonary embolism (pleuritic but with DVT risk/desaturation), a significant pericardial effusion progressing to cardiac tamponade (breathlessness, raised JVP, hypotension), and concurrent myocarditis (heart-failure signs, large troponin rise) - none of which is present here (no crushing pain, no rest dyspnoea/syncope, no leg swelling, no S3/raised JVP). Because the friction rub, ECG and troponin cannot be obtained over telehealth and ACS/myocarditis must be excluded, urgent in-person assessment is required before or alongside starting anti-inflammatory therapy, with clear safety-netting.

differential.evidence_basis: Sharp pleuritic chest pain worse lying flat and on inspiration, relieved by sitting forward, after a virus, is a classic pericarditis pattern.
Chest pain warrants excluding ACS; the sharp, positional, non-crushing nature is atypical for ischaemia, but ECG/troponin are needed to be sure.
Pleuritic pain warrants considering PE, but relief by sitting forward, trapezius radiation and (sealed) a friction rub favour pericarditis; DVT risk/desaturation would change this.
Considered for chest-wall pain, but a friction rub, fever and the profound positional nature (not simple palpation-reproducible pain) point to pericarditis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise the pericarditic pattern while arranging in-person ECG/troponin to exclude ACS/PE and concurrent myocarditis, and manage the pain effectively.

differential.evidence_basis: Positional pleuritic pain relieved by sitting forward (DI-001) with trapezius radiation (DI-002), a viral prodrome (DI-003), and no ACS/tamponade/myocarditis/PE features (DI-004), with (sealed) a pericardial friction rub, indicates uncomplicated acute pericarditis.
Still formally excluded with an ECG (widespread vs territorial ST changes) and troponin, but the clinical pattern is not ischaemic.
No DVT risk/desaturation; the positional pain with a friction rub is pericarditis.
A friction rub, fever and positional (not palpation-reproducible) pain are not costochondritis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is uncomplicated acute pericarditis: in-person ECG/troponin to exclude ACS/myocarditis, high-dose NSAIDs plus colchicine with gastric protection, and safety-netting for tamponade/myocarditis/ACS.

differential.diagnosis: Acute (viral) pericarditis - presumptive, pending in-person ECG (widespread ST elevation/PR depression), troponin and CRP
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation (friction rub, ECG) and exclusion of ACS/concurrent myocarditis require in-person assessment before/with starting NSAIDs and colchicine.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is likely acute pericarditis needing same-day in-person ECG/troponin and effective anti-inflammatory treatment, with clear escalation for ACS/tamponade/myocarditis features.

red_flags.description: Sharp pleuritic chest pain worse lying flat and on inspiration, relieved by sitting forward, with trapezius radiation
red_flags.clinical_significance: This positional pleuritic pattern with trapezius radiation is characteristic of pericardial inflammation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed pericarditis leaves severe pain untreated and a possible concurrent myocarditis/effusion unrecognised.

red_flags.description: Complication features (crushing pain, severe rest dyspnoea, syncope, leg swelling) - ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence argues against ACS, tamponade, myocarditis and PE and supports community management after in-person confirmation; if any develop, emergency assessment is needed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a complication develops and is not acted on, tamponade/myocarditis/ACS could be missed.

red_flags.description: Pericardial friction rub (high-pitched scratching, triphasic, loudest leaning forward in expiration) on clinician auscultation
red_flags.clinical_significance: A pericardial friction rub is the specific auscultatory sign of pericarditis; it cannot be assessed over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires auscultation; the correct action is in-person confirmation.

red_flags.description: Widespread (diffuse) concave ST-segment elevation and PR-segment depression on 12-lead ECG (with a normal/only mildly raised troponin) - pending
red_flags.clinical_significance: The diffuse ST elevation/PR depression pattern confirms pericarditis and distinguishes it from the territorial ST elevation of STEMI; troponin screens for concurrent myocarditis. These require in-person ECG/bloods.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires ECG/troponin; the correct action is in-person assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on a heart attack or a muscle strain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Positional pleuritic pain relieved by sitting forward is pericarditic, not the crushing pain of ACS nor simple palpation-reproducible costochondritis - but ACS must still be excluded with an ECG/troponin.
Pericarditis with heart-failure signs or a significant troponin rise is myopericarditis, which raises acuity; troponin and assessment for S3/raised JVP are needed.
A large pericardial effusion can progress to tamponade (breathlessness, raised JVP, hypotension); this must be safety-netted.
NSAIDs are first-line for pericarditis, but an ECG/troponin should confirm the diagnosis and exclude ACS and concurrent myocarditis before/with starting them.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for a pericardial friction rub
Troponin (to screen for concurrent myocarditis) and CRP
Assessment for a pericardial effusion/tamponade (JVP, blood pressure, echocardiography if needed)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care or emergency department) for a 12-lead ECG and troponin/CRP to confirm pericarditis and exclude acute coronary syndrome and concurrent myocarditis; then high-dose NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen with food) plus colchicine with gastric protection, and restriction of strenuous exercise - with emergency care if the pain becomes heavy/crushing, severe breathlessness at rest, fainting, or leg swelling develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring or starting NSAIDs without an in-person ECG/troponin risks missing an acute coronary syndrome or concurrent myocarditis, or a developing effusion/tamponade.
